subsystem: Ceph RADOS Gateway (RGW) S3 object-store frontend. This is RGWGetObj_ObjStore_S3::send_response_data, the method that serialises the HTTP response for an S3 GetObject request: it emits the status line, all response headers (content-type, content-length, etag, custom x-amz-meta-* metadata, object-lock/retention/legal-hold, tags), optional prepended sync metadata for system requests, and finally streams the object body back to the client.
provenance: Identified by class/method name RGWGetObj_ObjStore_S3::send_response_data (line 2), the RGW_* attribute macros (RGW_ATTR_ETAG, RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX, RGW_ATTR_TAGS, RGW_ATTR_OBJECT_RETENTION, RGW_SYS_PARAM_PREFIX), Ceph types (bufferlist, ldout/ldpp_dout/dendl, real_clock, utime_t, JSONFormatter, RGWObjTags/RGWObjectRetention/RGWObjectLegalHold), and the S3 REST helpers (dump_header, dump_errno, dump_etag, end_header, set_req_state_err). The banner names the file corpus/train/train_0116_cwe79.cpp; the header comment /* sample: primevul_validation_0383_fixed */ marks this as the post-fix revision. This is Ceph src/rgw/rgw_rest_s3.cc.
trust_boundary: The request state s and its query arguments (s->info.args) are attacker-controlled: a remote S3 client chooses the response-content-type / response-content-disposition / etc. override parameters (resp_attr_params, consumed at lines 103-105) and the request identity (line 109). The object's stored extended attributes (attrs, iterated at line 134) are also attacker-influenced because the same or another user set them at PUT time (x-amz-meta-*, tags, retention, ETag, content-type). Every value that reaches dump_header becomes a raw HTTP response header value, so the injection sink is the response header stream; the trust boundary is the point where these strings are copied into headers without control-character sanitisation.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The method takes bl, a bufferlist holding a chunk of the object body, plus bl_ofs and bl_len (both off_t, i.e. signed 64-bit) giving the offset into bl and the number of bytes to emit for this chunk. It is called repeatedly as the object is streamed. content_type is a raw char* that will eventually be handed to end_header; content_type_str is the std::string that owns the backing storage for that pointer (the classic .c_str() aliasing pair). response_attrs accumulates header-name -> header-value pairs to emit at the end, and metadata_bl holds optional JSON sync metadata for system requests.

Because the method is invoked once per body chunk, a member flag sent_header records whether the header block was already written. On the second and later calls it jumps straight to the send_data label (line 213) so headers are emitted exactly once and only the body is streamed thereafter. This goto-based state machine means all the header logic between here and line 211 runs only on the first chunk.

Emits the HTTP status. If the handler set a custom_http_ret it is dumped directly; otherwise the status is derived from op_ret, with a 206 Partial Content when partial_content is set and there was no error. set_req_state_err records the error state on s and dump_errno writes the numeric status/errno into the response.

If the operation failed (op_ret non-zero) the code skips all body-related header generation and jumps to the done label to finish the header block. Otherwise, if a Range request was made (range_str set) it emits a Content-Range header from start, end and the total object size s->obj_size.

For internal system requests (multi-site sync) that ask to prepend metadata, the code emits Rgwx-Object-Size, then JSON-encodes the object's attrs and mtime into a JSONFormatter, flushes them into metadata_bl, and advertises the embedded metadata length in a header. total_len (the byte count reported to the client) is grown by the metadata length, or zeroed when rgwx_stat means only metadata (no body) is returned. This branch is gated on s->system_request, an authenticated internal path, so it is less exposed than the public path below.

Still on the system-request path, this emits sync-related headers: the modification epoch (Rgwx-Mtime), the placement-group version (Rgwx-Obj-PG-Ver), and the source zone short id. decode_attr_bl_single_value decodes a fixed-width integer (uint64_t/uint32_t) out of a stored bufferlist attribute; a decode failure is logged and the default 0 is used rather than aborting. These header values are integers formatted by dump_header, so they are not a string-injection vector.

Emits the server-side-encryption response headers collected earlier in crypt_http_responses, then Content-Length (total_len), Last-Modified, and the SSE/version id header if present. It probes the object attrs for an append-part marker to advertise whether the object is Appendable, reporting the next append position from s->obj_size. version_id flows from stored/computed metadata into a header value.

On success, emits the ETag: either the large-object composite etag lo_etag, or the stored RGW_ATTR_ETAG attribute value converted to a string. The etag value ultimately originates from object metadata; dump_etag writes it as an HTTP header value. This whole success block extends to line 191.

This is the client-facing response-override machinery and the site of the security fix. resp_attr_params is a static table mapping S3 query params (response-content-type, response-content-disposition, response-content-language, response-expires, response-cache-control) to their HTTP header names (p->http_attr). For each, it reads the caller-supplied value from the query string via s->info.args.get. Two gates guard it: anonymous callers are rejected (is_anonymous, line 109) so that only authenticated identities can steer response headers, and str_has_cntrl(val) (line 119) rejects any value containing control characters (CR, LF, etc.), which is exactly the CRLF-injection / HTTP-response-splitting defence. If the param is response-content-type the value is stashed into content_type_str and content_type is pointed at its buffer; all other params go into response_attrs under their mapped header name.

Now the code walks every stored object attribute and turns the known ones into HTTP headers. For attrs listed in the rgw_to_http_attrs map (e.g. Content-Language, Cache-Control, Expires, Content-Disposition) it copies the value, trims trailing NUL padding bytes (the while loop at 143), and stores it in response_attrs unless a response param already set that header. RGW_ATTR_CONTENT_TYPE fills content_type only if not already set by the response-content-type override. RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX matches user custom metadata: name is advanced past the internal RGW_ATTR_PREFIX (line 161) so the header is emitted with the user-facing x-amz-meta-* name, and the raw attribute value iter->second is dumped as the header value. Crucially, none of these object-attribute values pass through str_has_cntrl the way the query-param path does.

The remaining attribute handlers decode structured, serialised metadata out of the stored bufferlist: tag count (RGWObjTags), object-lock retention mode and retain-until date (RGWObjectRetention), and legal-hold status (RGWObjectLegalHold). Each decode is wrapped in try/catch for buffer::error so that a malformed/truncated encoding is logged and skipped rather than crashing the request. get_mode/get_status return enumerated strings and count() an integer, so these emit controlled header values.

The done label flushes every accumulated header from response_attrs via dump_header, then closes the header block with end_header. If the object type was never determined content_type defaults to binary/octet-stream; note end_header is passed the raw content_type char* that aliases content_type_str, so that string must still be alive and unmodified at this point.

Finally, any prepended sync metadata is written as body, sent_header is latched true so subsequent calls skip straight here, and then the actual object payload is streamed: dump_body(s, bl.c_str() + bl_ofs, bl_len) emits bl_len bytes starting bl_ofs into the current bufferlist chunk. A negative return from dump_body (write error) is propagated; otherwise the method returns 0. This body path runs on every chunk once headers have been sent.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This dumps user-controlled custom metadata (x-amz-meta-*) straight into an HTTP response header value with no control-character filtering, unlike the query-param path which is gated by str_has_cntrl at line 119. iter->second is the raw stored attribute value, set by the client at PUT time. If a CR/LF (or NUL) survived ingest validation and was persisted, GET would re-emit it verbatim, splitting the header stream (HTTP response splitting / header injection, CWE-113/CWE-79) or truncating downstream headers. The safety of this path depends entirely on an invariant enforced elsewhere: that RGW rejects control characters in metadata at PUT. This same gap applies to the mapped-attr copy at line 147 (Content-Disposition/Content-Language/etc.) and the etag/version_id emissions. The correct defence is to validate header values at the sink (here) as well as at ingest, since GetObject only permits specific, well-formed headers.

This is the primary attack surface and the exact contract the fix addresses: an authenticated S3 client sets response-content-type / response-content-disposition / etc. and RGW reflects those values into HTTP response headers. Without the str_has_cntrl gate an attacker could embed CR/LF in a param value to inject arbitrary headers or a second response (CVE-2021-3524, HTTP header/response injection, CWE-113/CWE-79). The two gates must both hold: is_anonymous blocks unauthenticated callers from steering headers, and str_has_cntrl(val) must run BEFORE val is copied into response_attrs (line 126) or content_type_str (line 128). Confirm str_has_cntrl truly rejects both 0x0D/0x0A and other control bytes, and that no override param bypasses this loop. The gate is present here; the residual risk is that the equivalent object-attribute paths (finding above) are not similarly gated.

content_type is a raw char* aliasing content_type_str's internal buffer (also re-established at line 153 from rgw_bl_str). That pointer is later handed to end_header at line 205, and it must remain valid: if content_type_str were reassigned or resized after the .c_str() was captured while content_type still points at the old buffer, end_header would read freed/relocated storage (dangling pointer, use-after-free, CWE-416). In the current control flow the line-151 guard if (!content_type) prevents re-assigning content_type_str while a pointer is held, so it is safe as written, but the pattern is fragile: any future edit that mutates content_type_str after line 129/153 without resetting content_type reintroduces a UAF. The invariant is that content_type_str must outlive and not be modified after content_type is taken from it.

The body write does pointer arithmetic bl.c_str() + bl_ofs and reads bl_len bytes, with bl_ofs and bl_len being signed off_t supplied by the caller (range handling). If the caller ever passes bl_ofs or bl_len outside the bounds of the current bufferlist chunk (bl_ofs < 0, bl_ofs > bl.length(), or bl_ofs + bl_len > bl.length()), this is an out-of-bounds read of heap memory streamed to the client (information disclosure, CWE-125). Nothing in this function validates the two against bl.length(); the invariant that bl_ofs >= 0 and bl_ofs + bl_len <= bl.length() must be guaranteed by the caller (the range/read pipeline). Worth confirming the range computation upstream clamps these before this method is reached.

After matching the RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX prefix (strncmp over sizeof(RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX)-1 bytes), name is advanced by sizeof(RGW_ATTR_PREFIX)-1 and then used as a NUL-terminated header name. This is only safe if RGW_ATTR_PREFIX is a proper prefix of, and no longer than, RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX; otherwise name is advanced past the end of the matched string and dump_header reads out of bounds (CWE-125) or emits a truncated/garbage header name (CWE-193 off-by-one on the string length). Because both are compile-time macros this is a fixed relationship, so it is low risk, but confirm RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX == RGW_ATTR_PREFIX "..." so the advance never exceeds the key length.

The trailing-NUL trimming loop indexes s[len - 1] but guards with len && first, so len can never reach the s[len-1] access at 0; the size_t decrement cannot underflow and there is no out-of-bounds read here. This anchor is dismissed as safe, but it is worth noting the resulting value s is still emitted as a header value at line 147 without control-character validation, so it shares the same object-metadata CRLF-injection exposure described in the top finding rather than a distinct arithmetic bug.

summary: This is the post-fix revision of Ceph RGW's S3 GetObject response builder. The client-controlled response-* override parameters are now correctly gated against CRLF/header injection by an anonymous-identity check plus a str_has_cntrl filter (lines 109-119) — the CVE-2021-3524 fix. The residual, latent risk is that the object-metadata paths (custom x-amz-meta-* at line 162, mapped attrs at line 147, etag/version) reflect stored values into headers WITHOUT the same control-character validation, so header injection is still possible if any control character survives ingest; secondary concerns are the content_type .c_str() aliasing pattern and the unvalidated bl_ofs/bl_len body read.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: send_response_data(bufferlist& bl, off_t bl_ofs, off_t bl_len) signature
reviewed_anchors.reason: bl_ofs/bl_len are unvalidated signed offsets used in bl.c_str()+bl_ofs at line 215 (OOB read)
.c_str() pointer aliases a std::string handed to end_header; fragile UAF pattern (CWE-416)
one-shot header latch; deterministic state machine, no attacker influence on the branch
numeric status values via dump_errno; not a string-injection surface
emits a numeric Content-Range header from integer fields
authenticated internal sync path; total_len += metadata_bl.length() cannot realistically overflow uint64
fixed-width integer decode with error checked; emitted as integer headers
version_id/SSE headers share the general metadata-injection surface; covered by the object-attribute finding, no distinct arithmetic contract
etag value emitted as header; part of the same unvalidated-metadata surface, no separate contract
primary attacker-controlled response-header override surface
authorization gate for header override; must precede the copy — part of the injection contract
the CRLF-injection defence; must run before val is used and must cover all sinks
sink for the override value; feeds headers and the aliasing content_type pointer
iterates attacker-influenced stored metadata reflected into headers
mapped header value emitted without control-char validation (part of top injection finding); the while-loop arithmetic itself is guarded and safe
re-establishes the content_type .c_str() alias from stored metadata
emits constant header name/value 'True'; no attacker data in the header value
pointer advance assumes RGW_ATTR_PREFIX is no longer than the matched key; potential OOB/off-by-one

reviewed_anchors.anchor: user custom metadata dump_header(s, name, iter->second) (line 162)
reviewed_anchors.reason: raw x-amz-meta-* value emitted as header without control-char filtering — main CRLF-injection latent finding
untrusted serialized decode is bounded by try/catch(buffer::error); outputs are enum/int, not free-form injectable strings
flushes already-validated/collected values; end_header content_type aliasing is captured in the .c_str() finding
OOB read if bl_ofs/bl_len exceed bl bounds; no local validation

findings.title: HTTP header (CRLF) injection via unfiltered object metadata reflected into response headers
findings.anchor: dump_header(s, name, iter->second) for user custom metadata; response_attrs[aiter->second] = s for mapped attrs
findings.bug_class: HTTP response splitting / header injection (unvalidated metadata reflected into response headers)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — object attributes are set at PUT time; the sink is this method
findings.invariant: Every value written as an HTTP header value (mapped attrs, x-amz-meta-*, etag, version_id, content-type) must be free of control characters (CR/LF/NUL) — the str_has_cntrl gate applied to query params (line 119) must also cover values sourced from stored object metadata, or metadata must be provably control-char-free at ingest.
findings.failure_mode: The query-param override path is now filtered by str_has_cntrl, but the object-attribute loop (lines 134-190) reflects stored values into headers with no such filter. If a client persisted a custom metadata value or overridable attribute containing CR/LF (e.g. via a PUT path that did not sanitise it), a later GET emits it verbatim at line 147/162, letting the attacker inject arbitrary response headers or split the response (cache poisoning, XSS via injected Content-Type/body, session fixation). Impact is header/response injection served to any client fetching the object.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether RGW rejects control characters in x-amz-meta-* and in Content-Disposition/Content-Language/etc. at PUT; if not, str_has_cntrl (or equivalent) must be applied here at the dump_header sink for every metadata-sourced header value.

findings.title: Response-header override injection defence — verify the str_has_cntrl gate covers all override sinks
findings.bug_class: CRLF injection / HTTP response splitting via response-* query parameters
findings.invariant: For every response-* override parameter, the value must be rejected if it contains any control character BEFORE it is copied into response_attrs (line 126) or content_type_str (line 128); anonymous callers must be blocked from setting any override header.
findings.failure_mode: This is the exact vulnerability the fix closes: an authenticated client sets response-content-disposition (or another response-* param) to a value embedding %0d%0a<injected-header> and, absent the check, RGW would reflect it into the response header block, enabling response splitting and downstream XSS/cache poisoning. The gate is present and ordered correctly here; the finding is retained to require confirmation that str_has_cntrl catches 0x0D and 0x0A (and ideally all C0 controls) and that no override param bypasses this loop.
findings.what_to_confirm: Read str_has_cntrl to confirm it flags CR and LF specifically; confirm resp_attr_params has no entry emitted outside this validated loop.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds read of object body via unvalidated bl_ofs/bl_len offsets
findings.code: int r = dump_body(s, bl.c_str() + bl_ofs, bl_len);
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (pointer arithmetic on unvalidated signed offsets)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — the range/read pipeline that supplies bl_ofs and bl_len
findings.invariant: bl_ofs >= 0 and bl_ofs + bl_len <= bl.length() must hold for every call; the offset and length passed to dump_body must lie fully within the current bufferlist chunk.
findings.failure_mode: bl_ofs and bl_len are signed off_t and are used directly as bl.c_str() + bl_ofs with a bl_len-byte read, with no bounds check against bl.length() in this method. If the upstream range computation produces a negative or over-large offset/length, the server reads heap memory past the chunk and streams it to the client (information disclosure) or crashes. Safety depends entirely on the caller clamping the values.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the caller (range handling in RGWGetObj) validates and clamps bl_ofs/bl_len against the actual bufferlist length before this method runs.

findings.title: Fragile .c_str() aliasing: content_type points into content_type_str handed to end_header
findings.bug_class: Dangling pointer / use-after-free of std::string internal buffer
findings.invariant: content_type_str must remain alive and unmodified from the point content_type is assigned its .c_str() until end_header (line 205) has consumed it; no code path may reassign or resize content_type_str while content_type still aliases it.
findings.failure_mode: content_type holds a raw pointer into content_type_str's buffer (set at line 129 or 153). If content_type_str were later reassigned/resized while content_type still pointed at the old storage, end_header would dereference freed/relocated memory (CWE-416). As written, the guard if (!content_type) at line 151 prevents a second assignment while a pointer is held, so it is safe today; the risk is latent/maintenance — a future edit could break the invariant. Note this is the .c_str() lifetime pitfall from the reference notes.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no path between the .c_str() capture and end_header mutates content_type_str; consider passing content_type_str directly rather than a raw pointer.

findings.title: Pointer advance past custom-metadata key assumes RGW_ATTR_PREFIX <= RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX length
findings.bug_class: Off-by-one / out-of-bounds read from macro-length pointer arithmetic
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — depends on the RGW_ATTR_PREFIX / RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX macro definitions
findings.invariant: RGW_ATTR_PREFIX must be a prefix of RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX and no longer than it, so that advancing name by sizeof(RGW_ATTR_PREFIX)-1 never moves past the NUL of the matched attribute key.
findings.failure_mode: The branch is entered only when name starts with RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX (strncmp over sizeof(RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX)-1). name is then advanced by sizeof(RGW_ATTR_PREFIX)-1 and treated as a C string. If RGW_ATTR_PREFIX were longer than RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX the pointer would overshoot the key, producing an OOB read or an emitted garbage/truncated header name. Because both are compile-time constants this is a fixed, low-probability relationship, but it must be confirmed rather than assumed.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the macro definitions: RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX begins with RGW_ATTR_PREFIX and sizeof(RGW_ATTR_PREFIX) <= sizeof(RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX).

audit_checklist: Apply the same control-character validation used for response-* params (str_has_cntrl, line 119) to every metadata-sourced header value: custom x-amz-meta-* (line 162), rgw_to_http_attrs mapped values (line 147), etag and version_id.
Verify str_has_cntrl rejects both CR (0x0D) and LF (0x0A) — and ideally all C0/DEL control bytes — and runs before val is copied at lines 126/128.
Confirm bl_ofs and bl_len are clamped to [0, bl.length()] by the caller before dump_body(s, bl.c_str() + bl_ofs, bl_len) at line 215.
Confirm no code path reassigns or resizes content_type_str after content_type = content_type_str.c_str() (lines 129/153) and before end_header (line 205).
Confirm RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX starts with RGW_ATTR_PREFIX and sizeof(RGW_ATTR_PREFIX) <= sizeof(RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX) so the name += at line 161 cannot overshoot.
Confirm the RGWObjTags/RGWObjectRetention/RGWObjectLegalHold decode() paths cannot side-effect on partial data before the buffer::error is caught (lines 167/175/184).
Confirm that whether metadata is sanitised at PUT is the actual security boundary relied upon here, and document it, since this method trusts stored attribute values.
